serving.natural_language_portion: One large raw egg white weighs approximately 33 g and provides about 17 kcal with 3.6 g of protein.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, white, raw, fresh — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172183); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F01060007)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore database entry F01060007 used as primary source for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol. USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 172183 used to cross-validate and supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, water, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and vitamin values. Values are consistent between both sources within rounding tolerance.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — raw egg white contains negligible carbohydrates (0.73 g per 100 g); consulted University of Sydney GI Database which does not list a GI value for egg white due to negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw egg white contains only 0.73 g carbohydrate per 100 g, which is nutritionally negligible. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a GI value to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per Veritas q4 critical rule. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by convention for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (166 mg/100 g) offset by potassium (163 mg/100 g); near-zero saturated fat and zero cholesterol
hypertension.guidance: Raw egg white is suitable for hypertension management given its moderate sodium content of 166 mg per 100 g, meaningful potassium at 163 mg per 100 g providing a favourable Na:K ratio, and complete absence of saturated fat and cholesterol; individuals should avoid adding salt or high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or fish sauce during preparation to preserve this favourable cardiovascular profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.73 g/100 g) and high protein (10.9 g/100 g) with no measurable glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw egg white is highly suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its negligible carbohydrate content of 0.73 g per 100 g, which produces no measurable glycaemic response and no glycaemic load, while its high protein content of 10.9 g per 100 g supports satiety, lean muscle preservation, and post-meal glucose stability; it should always be cooked before consumption to neutralise avidin and maximise protein bioavailability.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), and high-quality lean complete protein (10.9 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw egg white presents a cardioprotective nutritional profile containing zero cholesterol, only 0.03 g saturated fat per 100 g, and 10.9 g of high-quality complete protein per 100 g, making it an excellent lean protein source for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk; it should be cooked and paired with vegetables and omega-3-rich foods rather than high-fat or high-sodium accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No meaningful anti-inflammatory factors present; no omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants, or dietary fibre
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw egg white does not meet the positive-criterion threshold for rheumatoid arthritis suitability as it contains no omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, antioxidants, or dietary fibre; it should be paired with anti-inflammatory foods such as oily fish, leafy greens, or turmeric to create an RA-suitable meal, and must always be cooked to neutralise avidin and maximise protein bioavailability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken egg white (raw) is the clear, viscous liquid portion of a chicken egg that surrounds the yolk, mechanically separated prior to any cooking or heat treatment. It is composed predominantly of water (approximately 87.6 g per 100 g) and high-quality complete protein (10.9 g per 100 g), primarily ovalbumin (~54%), ovotransferrin (~12%), ovomucoid (~11%), ovomucin (~3.5%), and lysozyme (~3.4%), all of which are bioavailable complete proteins containing all essential amino acids. In its raw state it is translucent, colourless to pale yellow, and gelatinous with a slightly slippery, viscous texture and a neutral, faintly saline taste. When heat-denatured through cooking, it becomes opaque, firm, and white, with a rubbery to tender texture depending on cooking method and temperature. It contains negligible fat (0.17 g per 100 g, of which only 0.03 g is saturated), negligible carbohydrate (0.73 g per 100 g), and zero cholesterol, making it one of the leanest high-protein whole foods available. Sodium is moderate at 166 mg per 100 g and potassium is 163 mg per 100 g, providing a reasonable Na:K ratio. It is a notable source of riboflavin (vitamin B2, 0.439 mg per 100 g) and selenium (20 mcg per 100 g). Raw egg white contains avidin, a glycoprotein that binds biotin (vitamin B7) with high affinity and inhibits its intestinal absorption; cooking fully denatures avidin and eliminates this antinutritional risk, making cooked egg white the strongly preferred form for regular consumption. Egg white is universally used across global cuisines and food applications: in baking for meringues, soufflés, macarons, and angel food cake; as a protein-enriching addition to smoothies and beverages; in clinical and sports nutrition protocols requiring high-protein, low-fat, zero-cholesterol dietary components; and as the base for egg white omelettes and frittatas. Clinically, it is highly suitable for type 2 diabetes (negligible glycaemic impact, no glycaemic load), hypertension (low saturated fat, moderate sodium offset by potassium), and cardiovascular disease (zero cholesterol, minimal saturated fat, high lean protein). It does not independently meet the positive anti-inflammatory criterion for rheumatoid arthritis suitability and should be paired with omega-3-rich or antioxidant-rich foods in that context. It is halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but not vegan. The food is classified as minimally processed — it is simply the separated albumen of a fresh chicken egg with no additives, preservatives, or processing agents.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F01060007, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source. Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172183, an approved secondary global source. Both sources are in close agreement within rounding tolerance. GI not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content (0.73 g/100 g), consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance for protein-dominant foods with negligible carbohydrates.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as egg white contains no dietary fibre. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as egg white contains no cholesterol — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172183 and HPB Singapore F01060007. trans_fat_g is naturally zero as egg white contains no trans fat. vitamin_c_mg is naturally zero as egg white contains no vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is naturally zero as egg white contains no vitamin A. vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as egg white contains no vitamin D. vitamin_e_mg is naturally zero as egg white contains no vitamin E. Saturated fat (0.03 g), monounsaturated fat (0.02 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.07 g), thiamine (0.004 mg), riboflavin (0.439 mg), niacin (0.105 mg), vitamin B6 (0.005 mg), vitamin B12 (0.09 mcg), folate (4 mcg DFE), phosphorus (15 mg), magnesium (11 mg), zinc (0.03 mg), selenium (20 mcg), and water (87.6 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 172183 to supplement HPB primary values. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.73 g/100 g); glycemic_index set to null (not 0) and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas q4 critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Raw egg white contains avidin which inhibits biotin absorption; cooking is strongly recommended before consumption. No multilingual local-language names are required as this is a generic global ingredient not specific to any single cuisine region requiring G6 mandatory multilingual coverage.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) per critical q4 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods — egg white has only 0.73 g carbohydrate per 100 g. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source fully populated with University of Sydney GI Database reference, clear source_match_notes explaining the not-applicable determination, and accessed_date.
